content.text: every token in a sequence attends to every other token in the same sequence
— including itself — to compute contextual representations. The word "self" means the attention is applied
a single sequence rather than between two different sequences (like a source and target in translation).
This is what gives Transformers their power: rather than processing tokens one-by-one (like RNNs), self-attention lets
all tokens communicate with all other tokens simultaneously
Why Self-Attention Matters in Generative AI
Self-attention is the core mechanism that enables modern AI models to understand context:
Any token can attend to any other, regardless of distance
Same word gets different representation depending on surrounding context
All tokens processed simultaneously (unlike sequential RNNs)
Model learns which words relate to which others
The classic example: in "The animal didn't cross the street because
was tired" — self-attention allows the model to learn that
by computing high attention between those two tokens.
The defining feature of self-attention is that
Queries, Keys, and Values all come from the same sequence

Regular attention (cross-attention):
  Q from sequence A  ←  different sources
  K and V from sequence B

Self-attention:
  Q from sequence X  ←  SAME source
  K from sequence X     for all three
  V from sequence X
between elements of the same sequence
, not between two different sequences. This is the fundamental distinction.
For every token in the sequence:
Step 1: Create Q, K, V from the input

Input: ["The", "cat", "sat"]
Embeddings: [e₁, e₂, e₃]

For each token, compute:
  Q = embedding × W_Q
  K = embedding × W_K
  V = embedding × W_V

Q = [Q_The, Q_cat, Q_sat]
K = [K_The, K_cat, K_sat]
V = [V_The, V_cat, V_sat]
Step 2: Compute attention scores for every token pair

Score(cat, The) = Q_cat · K_The
Score(cat, cat) = Q_cat · K_cat  ← token attending to itself
Score(cat, sat) = Q_cat · K_sat

Repeat for EVERY token in the sequence

Scaled score = score / √d_k

Softmax normalizes each row to sum to 1:
  [0.1, 0.7, 0.2]  ← "cat" pays 70% attention to itself,
                      20% to "sat", 10% to "The"

New representation of "cat" =
  0.1 × V_The + 0.7 × V_cat + 0.2 × V_sat
of "cat" that incorporates information from the whole sentence.

Input sequence: ["I", "bank", "by", "the", "river"]

Self-Attention for "bank":
  Q_bank compared against:
    K_I      → score 0.05
    K_bank   → score 0.20
    K_by     → score 0.10
    K_the    → score 0.05
    K_river  → score 0.60 ← high score! "river" helps clarify "bank"
  
  Softmax weights: [0.05, 0.20, 0.10, 0.05, 0.60]
  
  New "bank" representation =
    weighted sum of all V vectors
    → Encodes "bank" as RIVERBANK, not financial bank

Context changes meaning dynamically! 🎯
Each token sees all others simultaneously
Attention score depends on content, not position (positions added via positional encoding)
Attention weights change per input (not fixed like convolutions)
Every token attends to every token

Each token can attend to ALL other tokens — both before and after it:
  "bank" attends to: "I", "bank", "by", "the", "river"
  
Used in: BERT, encoders of BERT-like models

Each token can only attend to PREVIOUS tokens (future masked):
  "river" attends to: "I", "bank", "by", "the"
  "river" CANNOT see future tokens
  
Used in: GPT, autoregressive language models
During generation, the model doesn't know future tokens. Masking prevents information leakage from future positions.
Direct path between any two tokens, regardless of distance
Each token's output incorporates context from the whole sequence
All tokens computed simultaneously on GPUs
Attention weights visualizable to show what the model focuses on
attention matrix — expensive for very long sequences
Needs positional encoding to understand sequence position
Large attention matrices for long sequences
where every citizen can speak directly to every other citizen simultaneously:
: A game of telephone — tokens pass information one by one from left to right, losing quality at every step
: Every token gets its own microphone and can hear everyone else at once — no information decay, no waiting
reading a paragraph and building a personal summary for each word
Every word asks: "Which other words in this paragraph help explain my meaning?"
"Apple" might weight "fruit" and "eat" highly → means the food
"Apple" might weight "stock" and "market" highly → means the company
The diagram below shows the full data flow of multi-head self-attention from input embeddings to contextual output. Each stage maps to one line of the mathematical formulation.

attrs.alt: Multi-head self-attention data flow: input -> QKV projections -> score computation -> value aggregation -> output projection

content.text: Self-attention is defined by three learned linear projections applied to the same input matrix X, followed by a scaled dot-product, softmax normalisation, and value aggregation.
Project input X into query, key, and value matrices:

attrs.latex: Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V

content.text: prevents the dot products from growing large when
is large. Without it, softmax would enter near-zero gradient regions and training would stall.
Multi-head attention runs h independent attention heads in parallel and concatenates their outputs:
For causal (decoder) attention, a mask is applied before softmax:
before softmax forces their probability to zero, so position i can only attend to positions 0 through i.
When to Use Self-Attention vs Alternatives
Long-range dependencies matter — coreference across paragraphs, cross-sentence reasoning.
Training data is large enough for the quadratic cost to pay off in quality.
Parallelism during training is a priority over sequential processing.
The full context is available upfront (encoding tasks) or at each step (causal decoding).
state-space models (Mamba, S4) or linear attention offer near-linear scaling.
RNNs maintain a fixed-size recurrent state rather than a growing KV cache.
depthwise separable convolutions have lower arithmetic intensity than quadratic attention.
Define linear projections W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O — all without bias (standard practice).
Project the input X to Q, K, V; reshape to (batch, heads, seq, d_k) by splitting d_model into h heads.
Compute scaled scores: S = Q @ K.T / sqrt(d_k) giving shape (batch, heads, seq, seq).
Apply optional causal or padding mask by setting masked positions to -inf.
Apply row-wise softmax over the key dimension (dim=-1) to obtain attention weights A.
Compute weighted sum: Z = A @ V, then merge heads back to (batch, seq, d_model).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


class MultiHeadSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by num_heads"
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        self.num_heads = num_heads

        # Fused QKV projection is more efficient, but separate is clearer
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # (batch, seq, d_model) -> (batch, heads, seq, d_k)
        batch, seq, _ = x.shape
        return x.view(batch, seq, self.num_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def forward(
        self,
        x: torch.Tensor,
        mask: torch.Tensor | None = None,
    ) -> torch.Tensor:
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_q(x))   # (B, H, S, d_k)
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_k(x))
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_v(x))

        # Scaled dot-product attention
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
        attn = self.dropout(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1))

        # Weighted sum, merge heads
        out = torch.matmul(attn, V)                             # (B, H, S, d_k)
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(x.shape)   # (B, S, d_model)
        return self.W_o(out)


# Verify shapes
mha = MultiHeadSelfAttention(d_model=512, num_heads=8)
x = torch.randn(2, 16, 512)   # batch=2, seq_len=16, d_model=512
out = mha(x)
print(out.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 16, 512])

# Causal mask for autoregressive decoding
seq_len = 16
causal = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len)).unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)
out_causal = mha(x, mask=causal)
print(out_causal.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 16, 512])
Example 1: Flash Attention for Long Sequences

Standard attention materialises an n×n score matrix in GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM). Flash Attention (Dao et al. 2022) tiles the computation into SRAM-resident blocks, keeping memory at O(n) and achieving 2–4× wall-clock speedup on modern GPUs.

# Production pattern: Flash Attention via PyTorch 2.0+
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# F.scaled_dot_product_attention auto-selects Flash Attention on CUDA with fp16/bf16
batch, heads, seq, d_k = 4, 16, 2048, 64
Q = torch.randn(batch, heads, seq, d_k, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device='cuda')
K = torch.randn(batch, heads, seq, d_k, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device='cuda')
V = torch.randn(batch, heads, seq, d_k, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device='cuda')

# Causal decoder pass — O(n) memory, no score matrix materialised in HBM
out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)
print(out.shape)  # (4, 16, 2048, 64)

# For CPU or debugging: force math backend
with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(enable_flash=False, enable_math=True, enable_mem_efficient=False):
    out_math = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q.float(), K.float(), V.float(), is_causal=True)

# Difference is numerically tiny (fp32 vs bf16 rounding)
print("Max abs diff:", (out.float() - out_math).abs().max().item())
Example 2: Attention Head Probing with BERT
Each head in a trained transformer learns a different relationship. This example uses HuggingFace to extract attention weights and identify which head best links two occurrences of an ambiguous word.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased", output_attentions=True)

sentence = "The bank approved the loan application near the river bank"
inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(**inputs)

# 12 layers x 12 heads, each shape: (1, 12, seq, seq)
attn_all = out.attentions
tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])

# Find which head in layer 5 most strongly links the two "bank" mentions
layer5 = attn_all[5][0]   # (12 heads, seq, seq)
bank_indices = [i for i, t in enumerate(tokens) if t == "bank"]
print("bank tokens at positions:", bank_indices)

if len(bank_indices) >= 2:
    b1, b2 = bank_indices[0], bank_indices[1]
    head_scores = layer5[:, b1, b2]   # attention from first bank to second bank, per head
    best = head_scores.argmax().item()
    print(f"Layer 5, Head {best}: bank[0] -> bank[1] attention = {head_scores[best]:.3f}")
Real-World Example: KV Cache in Autoregressive Inference

During generation, a causal language model produces one token at a time. Recomputing K and V for the entire history at every step would cost O(n²) per token. In production (LLaMA, GPT-4, Mistral), a KV cache stores computed keys and values from previous steps, reducing per-step cost to O(n) for each new token.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


class KVCacheAttention(torch.nn.Module):
    """Single-head causal attention with a pre-allocated KV cache for autoregressive generation."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, max_len: int = 2048):
        super().__init__()
        self.d_model = d_model
        self.W_q = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_k = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_v = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        # Pre-allocate cache — avoids repeated torch.cat on the hot path
        self.register_buffer("k_cache", torch.zeros(1, max_len, d_model))
        self.register_buffer("v_cache", torch.zeros(1, max_len, d_model))
        self.cache_pos = 0

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """x: (1, 1, d_model) — single new token during generation."""
        q = self.W_q(x)   # (1, 1, d)
        k = self.W_k(x)
        v = self.W_v(x)

        # Write into cache at current position
        pos = self.cache_pos
        self.k_cache[:, pos : pos + 1, :] = k
        self.v_cache[:, pos : pos + 1, :] = v
        self.cache_pos += 1

        # Attend over all cached keys/values so far
        K = self.k_cache[:, :self.cache_pos, :]   # (1, pos+1, d)
        V = self.v_cache[:, :self.cache_pos, :]
        scores = torch.matmul(q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_model)  # (1, 1, pos+1)
        attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        return torch.matmul(attn, V)   # (1, 1, d)


# Simulate generating 10 tokens
model = KVCacheAttention(d_model=64, max_len=128)
for step in range(10):
    token = torch.randn(1, 1, 64)
    out = model(token)
    print(f"Step {step}: output shape {out.shape}, cache filled to {model.cache_pos}")
Training loss stalls; attention collapses to near-one-hot
Divide scores by math.sqrt(d_k) before softmax
Softmax on wrong axis (dim=-2 instead of dim=-1)
Each key gets a distribution over queries; output values are garbled
Always softmax(scores, dim=-1) — over the key dimension
Model attends to future tokens during training; fails at inference
Set is_causal=True or apply upper-triangle -inf mask before softmax
Using float32 QKV with Flash Attention kernel
RuntimeError or silent fallback to slow math backend
Cast Q, K, V to bfloat16 or float16 before calling sdp_kernel

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

d_k = 64
Q = torch.randn(2, 8, 16, d_k)
K = torch.randn(2, 8, 16, d_k)
V = torch.randn(2, 8, 16, d_k)

# --- Mistake 1: forgetting to scale ---
scores_wrong = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1))  # variance = d_k, not 1
# Standard deviation of unscaled scores ~ sqrt(d_k) = 8.0
print("Unscaled std:", scores_wrong.std().item())     # ~8

scores_right = scores_wrong / (d_k ** 0.5)
print("Scaled std:", scores_right.std().item())       # ~1.0

# --- Mistake 2: softmax on wrong dimension ---
attn_bug = F.softmax(scores_right, dim=-2)  # BUG: rows no longer sum to 1
attn_ok  = F.softmax(scores_right, dim=-1)  # correct: each query's distribution over keys

print("Bug row sums (should be 1.0):", attn_bug.sum(dim=-1).mean().item())  # != 1.0
print("OK  row sums (should be 1.0):", attn_ok.sum(dim=-1).mean().item())   # = 1.0
automatically dispatches to Flash Attention on CUDA with fp16/bf16, avoiding the O(n^2) memory materialisation for long sequences.
Set is_causal=True instead of hand-building masks:
the fused kernel generates and applies the causal mask internally without a separate allocation.
Use bfloat16 for training, float16 for inference:
bf16 has the same dynamic range as fp32 (important for score magnitude stability) while halving memory cost.
H = -sum_j A[i,j] log A[i,j] per head. Near-zero entropy signals attention collapse; near-log(n) entropy signals an uninformative head. Both warrant investigation.
Use Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) for large-batch inference:
GQA (LLaMA-3, Mistral) shares K and V heads across groups of Q heads, shrinking the KV cache by 4–8x with negligible quality loss.
Attention cost is the primary scalability constraint for transformers on long sequences. The table compares the standard implementation with hardware-optimised variants:
Full n x n matrix in HBM; bottleneck for long context
Avoids materialising score matrix; 2-4x wall-clock speedup
Single K,V shared across all Q heads; faster inference, slight quality loss
g KV head groups; LLaMA-3, Mistral, Gemma use this
Attends only within local window of size w; loses global context per layer
built-in since 2.0, auto-selects Flash Attention, memory-efficient, or math backends. Zero extra dependencies on CUDA.
reference implementation of Flash Attention 2 and 3; required by most open-source LLMs for production training.
provides memory-efficient attention and other building blocks; integrates with Diffusers and Stable Diffusion pipelines.
output_attentions=True returns per-layer, per-head attention tensors for every supported model. BertViz visualises them interactively.
makes head-split reshapes readable: rearrange(x, 'b s (h d) -> b h s d', h=num_heads) replaces the view+transpose chain.
Streaming, edge inference with fixed-size state

malicious text injected into a retrieved document can attract high attention scores from query tokens, steering the model toward attacker-controlled content. Mitigate with structural delimiters and output-stage filtering.
in multi-turn dialogue, K and V tensors from earlier turns persist. Adversarial earlier turns can plant attention-attracting tokens that influence later unrelated queries.
softmax in fp16 vs bfloat16 vs fp32 produces subtly different distributions at the tail. Fix the dtype and verify reproducibility for safety-critical applications.
abnormally low entropy (one position capturing near-100% attention) can signal prompt injection or jailbreak attempts and is worth logging in production.
Self-attention computes Q, K, V from the same input sequence, giving every token direct access to every other token in a single layer.
— the sqrt(d_k) scale is critical to prevent gradient saturation.
Causal masking sets future positions to -inf before softmax, enforcing autoregressive ordering in decoder-only models (GPT, LLaMA).
Multi-head attention runs h independent heads in parallel, each learning a different relationship (syntactic, coreference, positional).
The O(n^2) memory cost of the score matrix is the main long-context bottleneck; Flash Attention eliminates it through tiling.
F.scaled_dot_product_attention (PyTorch >= 2.0) auto-dispatches to Flash Attention on CUDA with fp16/bf16 — use it instead of hand-rolling softmax.
KV caches amortise the per-token cost of autoregressive generation to O(n) instead of O(n^2) by reusing previously computed keys and values.
Attention weights are an interpretability window but not a causal explanation; pair them with gradient attribution for rigorous analysis.
